title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement [INSERT CONTACT METHOD]. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.0, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/0/ code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https:// www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Contributing to edgebundle
text: want make bigger change, ’s good idea first file issue make sure someone team agrees ’s needed. ’ve found bug, please file issue illustrates bug minimal reprex (also help write unit test, needed).

title: Contributing to edgebundle
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("", fork = TRUE). Install development dependences devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

title: Contributing to edgebundle
text: New code follow tidyverse style guide. can use styler package apply styles, please don’t restyle code nothing PR. use roxygen2, Markdown syntax, documentation. use testthat unit tests. Contributions test cases included easier accept.

title: Choosing a bundling algorithm
text: also reachable single dispatcher:
code: edge_bundle(g, xy, type = "force")   # or "divided", "stub", "path", "hammer", "mingle"

title: Choosing a bundling algorithm
text: Force-directed ("force") classic Holten & van Wijk algorithm sensible default small medium graphs. O(E²) number edges, becomes slow large graphs. Divided ("divided") directed variant (Selassie et al. 2011). Edges running opposite directions pulled separate lanes instead merging, drawing shows asymmetry flow. Use edge direction matters pass directed graph. Stub ("stub") bundles parts edges near endpoints, keeps individual edges distinguishable — good fit geographic networks. Plot result ggforce::geom_bezier(). Edge-path ("path") routes edge along short path graph much detour (max_distortion). produces less ambiguous bundles force-directed bundling. Hammer ("hammer") bundles kernel density estimation. now native C++ implementation (Python needed) scales close linearly, making good choice large, dense graphs. MINGLE ("mingle") groups edges bottom-whenever routing together reduces total drawn length. scales best (O(E log E)) right choice large graphs.

title: Choosing a bundling algorithm
text: one--many flow data (weighted star graph), use flow map rather bundler. flow_tree() recommended layout: angle-restricted spiral tree planar, keeps node positions fixed, needs single parameter alpha. older tnss_tree() (triangulation + approximate Steiner tree) kept alternative requires interp package set dummy nodes.
code: xy <- cbind(state.center$x, state.center$y)[!state.name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii"), ] flow <- flow_tree(cali2010, xy, root = 4, alpha = 40)

title: Choosing a bundling algorithm
text: Since version 2.2.0, ggraph supports force-directed edge bundling natively via geom_edge_bundle_*(). edgebundle stays useful ggplot-agnostic toolkit (returns plain data frames) provides methods ggraph : divided/directed variant, stub, edge-path, MINGLE, Python-free hammer, flow map layouts.

title: Choosing a bundling algorithm
text: Edge bundling produces neat-looking visualizations necessarily improve readability, algorithms sensitive parameters. Consult original papers experiment — expect miracles.

what: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
title: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
text: R package implements several edge bundling/flow metro map algorithms. far includes Force directed edge bundling, divided variant directed graphs (paper) Stub bundling (paper) Hammer bundling via kernel density estimation (paper) — native C++, Python required Edge-path bundling (paper) MINGLE, multilevel agglomerative bundling (paper) Spiral-tree (paper) TNSS (paper) flow maps bundlers also reachable single edge_bundle(object, xy, type = ). ggraph 2.2.0 added native force-directed edge bundling via geom_edge_bundle_*(). edgebundle remains useful ggplot-agnostic toolkit returns plain data frames additionally provides methods ggraph : divided/directed bundling, stub, edge-path, MINGLE, Python-free hammer, flow maps.

title: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
text: package available CRAN. developer version can installed
code: install.packages("edgebundle") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("schochastics/edgebundle") library(edgebundle) library(igraph)

title: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
text: expected input edge bundling function graph (igraph/network tbl_graph object) node layout. functions return data frame points along edges network can plotted {{ggplot2}} using geom_path() geom_bezier() edge_bundle_stub(). result can visualized follows.   edge_bundle_stub(), need geom_bezier() package {{ggforce}}.  typical edge bundling benchmark uses dataset us flights, included package.
code: library(igraph) g <- graph_from_edgelist(     matrix(c(1, 12, 2, 11, 3, 10, 4, 9, 5, 8, 6, 7), ncol = 2, byrow = T), F ) xy <- cbind(c(rep(0, 6), rep(1, 6)), c(1:6, 1:6))  fbundle <- edge_bundle_force(g, xy, compatibility_threshold = 0.1) head(fbundle) #>            x        y      index group #> 1 0.00000000 1.000000 0.00000000     1 #> 2 0.00611816 1.199768 0.03030303     1 #> 3 0.00987237 1.297670 0.06060606     1 #> 4 0.01929293 1.524269 0.09090909     1 #> 5 0.02790686 1.686429 0.12121212     1 #> 6 0.03440142 1.812852 0.15151515     1 library(ggplot2)  ggplot(fbundle) +     geom_path(aes(x, y, group = group, col = as.factor(group)),         linewidth = 2, show.legend = FALSE     ) +     geom_point(data = as.data.frame(xy), aes(V1, V2), size = 5) +     theme_void() # simple edge-path bundling example g <- graph_from_edgelist(matrix(c(1, 2, 1, 6, 1, 4, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 6),     ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE ), FALSE) xy <- cbind(c(0, 10, 25, 40, 50, 50), c(0, 15, 25, 15, 0, -10)) res <- edge_bundle_path(g, xy, max_distortion = 2, weight_fac = 2, segments = 50)  ggplot() +     geom_path(         data = res, aes(x, y, group = group, col = as.factor(group)),         linewidth = 2, show.legend = FALSE     ) +     geom_point(data = as.data.frame(xy), aes(V1, V2), size = 5) +     scale_color_manual(values = c("grey66", "firebrick3", "firebrick3", rep("grey66", 4))) +     theme_void() library(ggforce) g <- make_star(10, "undirected")  xy <- matrix(c(     0, 0,     cos(90 * pi / 180), sin(90 * pi / 180),     cos(80 * pi / 180), sin(80 * pi / 180),     cos(70 * pi / 180), sin(70 * pi / 180),     cos(330 * pi / 180), sin(330 * pi / 180),     cos(320 * pi / 180), sin(320 * pi / 180),     cos(310 * pi / 180), sin(310 * pi / 180),     cos(210 * pi / 180), sin(210 * pi / 180),     cos(200 * pi / 180), sin(200 * pi / 180),     cos(190 * pi / 180), sin(190 * pi / 180) ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)  sbundle <- edge_bundle_stub(g, xy, beta = 90)  ggplot(sbundle) +     geom_bezier(aes(x, y, group = group), linewidth = 1.5, col = "grey66") +     geom_point(data = as.data.frame(xy), aes(V1, V2), size = 5) +     theme_void() g <- us_flights xy <- cbind(V(g)$longitude, V(g)$latitude) verts <- data.frame(x = V(g)$longitude, y = V(g)$latitude)  fbundle <- edge_bundle_force(g, xy, compatibility_threshold = 0.6) sbundle <- edge_bundle_stub(g, xy) hbundle <- edge_bundle_hammer(g, xy, bw = 0.7, decay = 0.5) pbundle <- edge_bundle_path(g, xy, max_distortion = 12, weight_fac = 2, segments = 50)  states <- map_data("state")   p1 <- ggplot() +     geom_polygon(         data = states, aes(long, lat, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.1, fill = NA     ) +     geom_path(         data = fbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "#9d0191", linewidth =  0.05     ) +     geom_path(         data = fbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.005     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "#9d0191", size = 0.25     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.5     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts[verts$name != "", ],         aes(x, y), col = "white", size = 3, alpha = 1     ) +     labs(title = "Force Directed Edge Bundling") +     ggraph::theme_graph(background = "black") +     theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"))  p2 <- ggplot() +     geom_polygon(         data = states, aes(long, lat, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.1, fill = NA     ) +     geom_path(         data = hbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "#9d0191", linewidth =  0.05     ) +     geom_path(         data = hbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.005     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "#9d0191", size = 0.25     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.5     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts[verts$name != "", ], aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 3, alpha = 1     ) +     labs(title = "Hammer Edge Bundling") +     ggraph::theme_graph(background = "black") +     theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"))  alpha_fct <- function(x, b = 0.01, p = 5, n = 20) {     (1 - b) * (2 / (n - 1))^p * abs(x - (n - 1) / 2)^p + b }  p3 <- ggplot() +     geom_polygon(         data = states, aes(long, lat, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.1, fill = NA     ) +     ggforce::geom_bezier(         data = sbundle, aes(x, y,             group = group,             alpha = alpha_fct(..index.. * 20)         ), n = 20,         col = "#9d0191", linewidth =  0.1, show.legend = FALSE     ) +     ggforce::geom_bezier(         data = sbundle, aes(x, y,             group = group,             alpha = alpha_fct(..index.. * 20)         ), n = 20,         col = "white", linewidth =  0.01, show.legend = FALSE     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "#9d0191", size = 0.25     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.5     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts[verts$name != "", ], aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 3, alpha = 1     ) +     labs(title = "Stub Edge Bundling") +     ggraph::theme_graph(background = "black") +     theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"))  p4 <- ggplot() +     geom_polygon(         data = states, aes(long, lat, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.1, fill = NA     ) +     geom_path(         data = pbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "#9d0191", linewidth =  0.05     ) +     geom_path(         data = pbundle, aes(x, y, group = group),         col = "white", linewidth =  0.005     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "#9d0191", size = 0.25     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts, aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.5     ) +     geom_point(         data = verts[verts$name != "", ], aes(x, y),         col = "white", size = 3, alpha = 1     ) +     labs(title = "Edge-Path Bundling") +     ggraph::theme_graph(background = "black") +     theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"))  p1 p2 p3 p4

title: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
text: flow map type thematic map represent movements. may thus considered hybrid map flow diagram. recommended layout flow_tree(), angle-restricted spiral tree: planar, keeps node positions fixed, needs dummy nodes, controlled single angle parameter. package also implements older TNSS layout based triangulation approximate Steiner trees. function tnss_tree() expects one--many flow network (.e. weighted star graph), layout nodes set dummy nodes created tnss_dummies().  smooth tree, use tnss_smooth(). Note changes object type need visualize {{ggplot2}} rather {{ggraph}}.
code: xy <- cbind(state.center$x, state.center$y)[!state.name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii"), ] flow <- flow_tree(cali2010, xy, root = 4, alpha = 40) library(ggraph) xy <- cbind(state.center$x, state.center$y)[!state.name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii"), ] xy_dummy <- tnss_dummies(xy, 4) gtree <- tnss_tree(cali2010, xy, xy_dummy, 4, gamma = 0.9)  ggraph(gtree, "manual", x = V(gtree)$x, y = V(gtree)$y) +     geom_polygon(data = us, aes(long, lat, group = group), fill = "#FDF8C7", col = "black") +     geom_edge_link(aes(width = flow, col = sqrt((xy[root, 1] - ..x..)^2 + (xy[root, 2] - ..y..)^2)),         lineend = "round", show.legend = FALSE     ) +     scale_edge_width(range = c(0.5, 4), trans = "sqrt") +     scale_edge_color_gradient(low = "#cc0000", high = "#0000cc") +     geom_node_point(aes(filter = tnss == "leaf"), size = 1) +     geom_node_point(aes(filter = (name == "California")), size = 5, shape = 22, fill = "#cc0000") +     theme_graph() +     labs(title = "Migration from California (2010) - Flow map") smooth_df <- tnss_smooth(gtree, bw = 5, n = 20)  ggplot() +     geom_polygon(data = us, aes(long, lat, group = group), fill = "#FDF8C7", col = "black") +     geom_path(         data = smooth_df, aes(x, y, group = destination, linewidth =  flow),         lineend = "round", col = "firebrick3", alpha = 1     ) +     theme_void() +     scale_size(range = c(0.5, 3), guide = "none") +     labs(title = "Migration from California (2010) - Flow map smoothed")

title: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps
text: Metro map(-like) graph drawing follow certain rules, octilinear edges. algorithm implemented packages uses hill-climbing optimize several features desired metro map. package includes metro map Berlin example. Note: metro_multicriteria() deprecated removed future release. Use graphlayouts::layout_as_metromap() instead.
code: # the algorithm has problems with parallel edges g <- simplify(metro_berlin) xy <- cbind(V(g)$lon, V(g)$lat) * 100  # the algorithm is not very stable. try playing with the parameters xy_new <- metro_multicriteria(g, xy, l = 2, gr = 0.5, w = c(100, 100, 1, 1, 100), bsize = 35)  # geographic layout ggraph(metro_berlin, "manual", x = xy[, 1], y = xy[, 2]) +     geom_edge_link0(aes(col = route_I_counts), edge_width = 2, show.legend = FALSE) +     geom_node_point(shape = 21, col = "white", fill = "black", size = 3, stroke = 0.5)  # schematic layout ggraph(metro_berlin, "manual", x = xy_new[, 1], y = xy_new[, 2]) +     geom_edge_link0(aes(col = route_I_counts), edge_width = 2, show.legend = FALSE) +     geom_node_point(shape = 21, col = "white", fill = "black", size = 3, stroke = 0.5) +     theme_graph() +     labs(title = "Subway Network Berlin")

title: Edge bundling — edge_bundle

title: Edge bundling — edge_bundle
text: object graph object (igraph/network/tbl_graph) xy coordinates vertices type bundling algorithm: "force" (force-directed, Holten), "divided" (force-directed directed graphs, Selassie et al.), "stub" (Nocaj & Brandes), "path" (edge-path, Wallinger et al.), "hammer" (KDE, Hurter et al.), "mingle" (Gansner et al.) ... arguments passed selected edge_bundle_*() function

title: Edge bundling — edge_bundle
text: data.frame containing bundled edges (x, y, index, group)

title: Edge bundling — edge_bundle
text: David Schoch

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: Implements classic edge bundling Holten.

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
code: edge_bundle_force(   object,   xy,   K = 1,   C = 6,   P = 1,   S = 0.04,   P_rate = 2,   I = 50,   I_rate = 2/3,   compatibility_threshold = 0.6,   eps = 1e-08,   directed = FALSE,   ... )

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: object graph object (igraph/network/tbl_graph) xy coordinates vertices K spring constant C number iteration cycles P number initial edge divisions S initial step size P_rate rate edge divisions number initial iterations I_rate rate iteration decrease per cycle compatibility_threshold threshold edges considered compatible eps accuracy directed logical. TRUE, use divided edge bundling (Selassie et al. 2011) keeps edges running opposite directions separate lanes. Requires igraph/tbl_graph object edge directions. ... arguments passed divided bundler directed = TRUE: lane_width, k_spring, k_charge, decay, friction, step, passes, iterations, use_connectivity.

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: data.frame containing bundled edges

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: re-implementation https://github.com/upphiminn/d3.ForceBundle. Force directed edge bundling slow (O(E^2)). see online plotting tips

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: Holten, Danny, Jarke J. Van Wijk. "Force-Directed Edge Bundling Graph Visualization." Computer Graphics Forum (Blackwell Publishing Ltd) 28, . 3 (2009): 983-990. Selassie, David, Brandon Heller, Jeffrey Heer. "Divided edge bundling directional network data." IEEE Transactions Visualization Computer Graphics 17, . 12 (2011): 2354-2363.

title: force directed edge bundling — edge_bundle_force
text: David Schoch

title: hammer edge bundling — edge_bundle_hammer
text: object graph object (igraph/network/tbl_graph) xy coordinates vertices bw initial bandwidth (fraction layout extent) decay bandwidth decay per iteration (0-1) npoints number points sampled per edge iterations number bundling iterations grid resolution density grid step advection step size (multiple bandwidth) smooth number smoothing passes per iteration

title: hammer edge bundling — edge_bundle_hammer
text: data.frame containing bundled edges

title: hammer edge bundling — edge_bundle_hammer
text: Native re-implementation KDE-based bundling behind datashader "hammer" bundler, following Hurter, Ersoy Telea (2012). Earlier versions function wrapped datashader reticulate; now runs entirely C++ Python dependency. Edges sampled points, density field estimated grid, points advected density gradient, bandwidth shrinks iteration bundles emerge along density ridges. see online plotting tips

title: hammer edge bundling — edge_bundle_hammer
text: Hurter, Christophe, Ozan Ersoy, Alexandru Telea. "Graph Bundling Kernel Density Estimation." Computer Graphics Forum 31, . 3 (2012): 865-874.

title: hammer edge bundling — edge_bundle_hammer
text: David Schoch

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: Multilevel agglomerative edge bundling (MINGLE).

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
code: edge_bundle_mingle(object, xy, k = 10, segments = 50, bundle_strength = 0.9)

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: object graph object (igraph/network/tbl_graph) xy coordinates vertices k number nearest neighbours considered merge candidates per edge segments number points sampled per bundled edge bundle_strength strength bundling 0 (straight edges) 1 (route fully meeting points)

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: data.frame containing bundled edges

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: Groups edges bottom-: two bundles merged whenever routing shared meeting points reduces total drawn length ("ink"). Meeting points geometric medians source-side target-side endpoints. kNN proximity graph edges built kd-tree coarsened level level (O(E log E)). see online plotting tips

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: Gansner, Emden R., Yifan Hu, Stephen North, Carlos Scheidegger. "Multilevel agglomerative edge bundling visualizing large graphs." 2011 IEEE Pacific Visualization Symposium (2011): 187-194.

title: MINGLE edge bundling — edge_bundle_mingle
text: David Schoch

what: edgebundle: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps — edgebundle-package
title: edgebundle: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps — edgebundle-package
text: R package implements several edge bundling/flow metro map algorithms. includes: Force directed edge bundling edge_bundle_force() (divided/directed variant) Stub bundling  edge_bundle_stub() Hammer bundling (KDE) edge_bundle_hammer() Edge-path bundling  edge_bundle_path() MINGLE bundling  edge_bundle_mingle() TNSS flow map tnss_*() Multicriteria Metro map layout metro_multicriteria()

title: edgebundle: Algorithms for Bundling Edges in Networks and Visualizing Flow and Metro Maps — edgebundle-package
text: Maintainer: David Schoch david@schochastics.net (ORCID) Authors: David Schoch david@schochastics.net (ORCID) contributors: Jose Luis Blanco-Claraco (Author bundled nanoflann C++ library) [copyright holder]

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
text: Computes one--many flow map angle-restricted spiral tree (Verbeek, Buchin Speckmann 2011). recommended flow map layout; unlike tnss_tree needs dummy nodes triangulation, keeps real node positions fixed, produces planar (crossing-free) tree smooth, shallow-angle merges, tuned single parameter alpha.

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
code: flow_tree(object, xy, root, alpha = 40, n = 20, optimize = FALSE)

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
text: object one--many flow network (star graph) igraph/tbl_graph, weight edge attribute giving flow xy coordinates vertices root root node id flow alpha restricting angle degrees (0-90). Smaller values bundle tightly toward root; typical values 20-45. n number points sampled per tree edge optimize logical. TRUE, refine tree approximate FLOWTREE optimization (Verbeek et al. 2011, section 5): join points stay fixed edge interiors relaxed smoothness keep clear node obstacles. can slightly relax strict alpha bound.

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
text: data.frame columns x, y, flow edge (one smooth arc per tree edge)

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
text: Verbeek, Kevin, Kevin Buchin, Bettina Speckmann. "Flow map layout via spiral trees." IEEE Transactions Visualization Computer Graphics 17, . 12 (2011): 2536-2545.

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
text: David Schoch

title: Spiral flow tree — flow_tree
code: xy <- cbind(state.center$x, state.center$y)[!state.name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii"), ] flow <- flow_tree(cali2010, xy, root = 4, alpha = 40)

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: Metro map layout based multicriteria optimization. Deprecated: use graphlayouts::layout_as_metromap() instead, now provides layout.

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
code: metro_multicriteria(object, xy, l = 2, gr = 0.0025, w = rep(1, 5), bsize = 5)

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: object original graph xy initial layout original graph l desired multiple grid point spacing. (l*gr determines desired edge length) gr grid spacing. (l*gr determines desired edge length) w weight vector criteria (see details) bsize number grid points station can move away rom original position

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: new coordinates stations

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: function optimizes following five criteria using hill climbing algorithm: Angular Resolution Criterion: angles incident edges station maximized, small angle two adjacent edges, can become difficult distinguish Edge Length Criterion: edge lengths across whole map approximately equal ensure regular spacing stations. based preferred multiple, l, grid spacing, g. purpose criterion penalize edges longer shorter lg. Balanced Edge Length Criterion: length edges incident particular station similar Line Straightness Criterion: (yet implemented) Edges form part line , possible, co-linear either side station line passes Octiinearity Criterion: edge drawn horizontally, vertically, diagonally 45 degree, penalize edges desired angle see online plotting tips

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: Stott, Jonathan, et al. "Automatic metro map layout using multicriteria optimization." IEEE Transactions Visualization Computer Graphics 17.1 (2010): 101-114.

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
text: David Schoch

title: Metro Map Layout (deprecated) — metro_multicriteria
code: # the algorithm has problems with parallel edges library(igraph) g <- simplify(metro_berlin) xy <- cbind(V(g)$lon, V(g)$lat) * 100  # the algorithm is not very stable. try playing with the parameters xy_new <- metro_multicriteria(g, xy, l = 2, gr = 0.5, w = c(100, 100, 1, 1, 100), bsize = 35) #> Warning: metro_multicriteria() is deprecated; use graphlayouts::layout_as_metromap() instead.

title: Sample points for triangulated networks — tnss_dummies
text: xy coordinates "real" nodes root root node id circ logical. create circular dummy nodes around leafs. line logical. create dummy nodes straight line root leafs. diag logical. create dummy nodes diagonally space. grid logical. create dummy nodes grid. rand logical. create random dummy nodes. ncirc numeric. number circular dummy nodes per leaf. rcirc numeric. radius circles around leaf nodes. nline numeric. number straight line nodes per leaf. ndiag numeric. number dummy nodes diagonals. ngrid numeric. number dummy nodes per dim grid. nrand numeric. number random nodes create.

what: Create Steiner tree from real and dummy points — tnss_tree
title: Create Steiner tree from real and dummy points — tnss_tree
text: Creates approximated Steiner tree flow map visualization. purposes flow_tree now recommended flow map layout (planar, angle-restricted, dummy nodes); tnss_tree kept alternative requires interp package triangulation.

title: Create Steiner tree from real and dummy points — tnss_tree
code: tnss_tree(   g,   xy,   xydummy,   root,   gamma = 0.9,   epsilon = 0.3,   elen = Inf,   order = "random" )

title: Create Steiner tree from real and dummy points — tnss_tree
text: g original flow network (must one--many flow network, .e star graph). Must weight attribute indicating flow xy coordinates "real" nodes xydummy coordinates "dummy" nodes root root node id flow gamma edge length decay parameter epsilon percentage points keept line straightening Visvalingam Algorithm elen maximal length edges triangulation order order shortest paths calculated ("random","weight","near","far")

title: Create Steiner tree from real and dummy points — tnss_tree

title: edgebundle 1.0.0
text: added edge_bundle(), single entry point dispatches bundler via type = c("force", "divided", "stub", "path", "hammer", "mingle") switched test suite testthat edition 3 added correctness tests every exported function modernised igraph calls 2.x API, removing deprecation warnings edge_bundle_force() now also accepts two-column edgelist matrix fixed edge_bundle_stub(): angular grouping broken (wrong circular-gap handling bundle-size cap summed cluster ids instead counting edges) vertical edges produce NaN. Bundling output changes result fixed edge_bundle_path(): path-length used distortion test computed along wrong vertices, edges essentially never routed. Bundling output changes result added divided edge bundling directed graphs via edge_bundle_force(directed = TRUE) (Selassie et al. 2011): edges running opposite directions kept separate lanes edge_bundle_hammer() now native C++ implementation KDE edge bundling (Hurter et al. 2012) longer depends Python/reticulate/datashader. reticulate dropped Imports install_bundle_py() removed. Bundling output differs datashader-based version added edge_bundle_mingle(), multilevel agglomerative edge bundling (Gansner et al. 2011); kNN proximity graph built bundled kd-tree (nanoflann) O(E log E) scaling, k parameter number merge candidates per edge metro_multicriteria() deprecated favour graphlayouts::layout_as_metromap() removed future release added flow_tree(), spiral-tree flow map (Verbeek, Buchin & Speckmann 2011): planar, angle-restricted, keeps node positions fixed, needs dummy nodes triangulation. recommended flow map layout; tnss_*() functions remain alternative. flow_tree(optimize = TRUE) additionally refines tree (smoothness + obstacle avoidance) join points held fixed tnss_tree(): order = "weight" now orders leaves flow magnitude (previously -op); fixed bug tnss_dummies() one diagonal corner misplaced; removed dead code. interp moved Imports Suggests (tnss_tree() needs )
